name: c
description: Modern C conventions, safety hardening, memory management, testing, and tooling for production C code

decisions.decision: Target the latest stable C standard (C23) with C17 as fallback for portability
decisions.rationale: C23 adds critical safety features: nullptr (type-safe null), [[nodiscard]], bool/true/false as keywords, typeof, auto, constexpr, and standard attributes. GCC defaults to C23 in recent versions. C17 fallback covers older toolchains.
decisions.details: Use -std=c23 as the default. Guard C23-specific features with __STDC_VERSION__ >= 202311L for portability. Key C23 features to adopt: nullptr (replaces NULL), [[nodiscard]] on error-returning functions, [[maybe_unused]], [[fallthrough]], [[deprecated]], unreachable() macro, binary literals (0b1010), digit separators.

decisions.decision: Enable comprehensive warnings in development; use -Werror=<specific> for critical warnings in distributed builds
decisions.rationale: Warnings catch real bugs at compile time. -Wall -Wextra alone miss important checks. Specific -Werror= flags catch critical issues without breaking builds on new compiler versions.
decisions.details:
Development baseline: -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wconversion -Wsign-conversion -Wformat=2 -Wimplicit-fallthrough -Wdouble-promotion -Wshadow -Wundef -Wstrict-prototypes -Wmissing-prototypes.

Always error on: -Werror=format-security -Werror=implicit -Werror=incompatible-pointer-types -Werror=int-conversion -Werror=return-type.

Do NOT distribute -Werror blanket — it creates a dependency on specific compiler versions. Use -Werror only in CI.

decisions.decision: Apply the full OpenSSF compiler hardening set in release builds
decisions.rationale: The OpenSSF Compiler Options Hardening Guide is the authoritative reference for production C code. These flags defend against buffer overflows, stack smashing, ROP/JOP attacks, and other exploits with minimal performance impact.
decisions.details:
Compile-time: -O2 -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=3 -fstack-clash-protection -fstack-protector-strong -ftrivial-auto-var-init=zero -fno-delete-null-pointer-checks -fno-strict-overflow -fno-strict-aliasing.

Position-independent: -fPIE -pie (executables), -fPIC -shared (libraries).

Linker: -Wl,-z,nodlopen -Wl,-z,noexecstack -Wl,-z,relro -Wl,-z,now -Wl,--as-needed.

Architecture: -fcf-protection=full (x86_64), -mbranch-protection=standard (ARM64).

GCC 15+ shortcut: -fhardened applies a predetermined hardening set in one flag.

decisions.decision: Return error codes with out-parameters; use [[nodiscard]] on all error-returning functions; goto-cleanup for multi-resource functions
decisions.rationale: C has no exceptions. Return codes are the universal pattern (POSIX, Linux kernel). [[nodiscard]] (C23) forces callers to check return values at compile time. The goto-cleanup pattern (CERT C MEM12-C) handles multi-resource cleanup safely.
decisions.details:
Primary pattern: return error enum, output via pointer parameter. Define a project-wide error enum (ERR_OK = 0, ERR_INVALID_INPUT, ERR_OUT_OF_MEMORY, etc.).

Multi-resource cleanup: goto-cleanup with resources freed in reverse acquisition order.

Consider tagged union result types for complex APIs where errors carry data.

Never return nil/NULL as 'not found' without an accompanying error code.

decisions.decision: Use __attribute__((cleanup)) macros for automatic resource management; arena allocation for batch work
decisions.rationale: The cleanup attribute provides RAII-like automatic resource cleanup in C — when a variable goes out of scope, the cleanup function is called even on early returns. The Linux kernel adopted this pattern in 2023. Arena allocation eliminates individual free() calls for request-scoped work.
decisions.details:
Define cleanup macros: _cleanup_(func) __attribute__((cleanup(func))). Provide convenience wrappers: _autofree_ for malloc'd pointers, _autoclose_ for file descriptors.

Do NOT mix goto-cleanup and cleanup attributes in the same function.

For portable code (MSVC), fall back to goto-cleanup.

Use -ftrivial-auto-var-init=zero to zero-initialize stack variables, preventing information leaks from uninitialized memory.

Document ownership transfer explicitly. Use naming conventions: *_new() allocates, *_free() releases.

decisions.decision: Mandate ASan+UBSan in CI for all test runs; TSan for threaded code; Valgrind for deep analysis
decisions.rationale: Sanitizers catch memory errors, undefined behavior, and data races that tests alone miss. ASan+UBSan has ~2-3x overhead and catches the most common bugs. TSan catches data races invisible to other tools.
decisions.details:
CI matrix:
1. ASan + UBSan (always): -fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -O1 -g
2. TSan (for threaded code, separate job): -fsanitize=thread
3. MSan (Clang-only, optional): -fsanitize=memory — requires ALL dependencies instrumented
4. Valgrind (optional): deep analysis, 10-50x overhead but catches things sanitizers miss

ASan, TSan, and MSan are mutually exclusive — run as separate CI jobs.

Set ASAN_OPTIONS=detect_leaks=1:abort_on_error=1 and UBSAN_OPTIONS=print_stacktrace=1:halt_on_error=1.

decisions.decision: Run clang-tidy and cppcheck in CI; enable CERT C checks in clang-tidy
decisions.rationale: clang-tidy and cppcheck are free, complementary, and CI-friendly. clang-tidy uses the compilation database for accurate analysis. cppcheck has a low false-positive rate. Together they catch different classes of bugs.
decisions.details:
clang-tidy config (.clang-tidy): enable bugprone-*, cert-*, clang-analyzer-*, misc-*, performance-*, portability-*, readability-*.

Generate compile_commands.json from CMake (-DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON) or Meson (automatic).

cppcheck: run with --enable=all --error-exitcode=1 --suppress=missingIncludeSystem.

Consider CodeChecker for a web dashboard over clang-tidy results with differential analysis.

decisions.decision: Use CMocka 2.0 for testing; it provides integrated type-safe mocking with no external dependencies
decisions.rationale: CMocka 2.0 (released December 2025) is the leading C test framework. It has built-in type-safe mocking macros (no code generation or Ruby needed), TAP 14 output, and first-class CMake and Meson support. Used by samba, libssh, coreboot, OpenVPN.
decisions.details:
CMocka requires only C99. Provides setup/teardown functions, group fixtures, type-safe assertions, and mocking via expect_* and will_return macros.

Alternative: Unity + CMock for embedded (requires Ruby for mock generation).

Organise one test file per module in tests/. Run via CTest (CMake) or Meson test runner.

decisions.decision: Use clang-format with a project-specific .clang-format config; enforce in CI
decisions.rationale: clang-format is the universal standard for C formatting. No serious alternative exists. Committed config eliminates formatting debates. CI enforcement prevents style drift.
decisions.details:
Place .clang-format at project root. Start from a base style: BasedOnStyle: LLVM (or Linux for kernel-style). Customize minimally (IndentWidth, ColumnLimit, PointerAlignment, BreakBeforeBraces).

CI: clang-format --dry-run --Werror (non-zero exit on diff).

Use // clang-format off / // clang-format on for exceptional blocks. Use git blame --ignore-revs-file to skip bulk-reformat commits.

decisions.decision: Use snake_case throughout; prefix all public API functions with the module name; SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for constants and macros
decisions.rationale: The C ecosystem universally uses snake_case (stdlib, POSIX, Linux kernel). Module prefixes prevent naming collisions and immediately signal the data domain.
decisions.details:
Variables and functions: snake_case (buffer_size, config_parse).
Types: snake_case_t suffix (config_t, error_t).
Constants and macros: SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE (MAX_BUFFER_SIZE, ERR_OK).
Enum values: SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE (PARSE_OK, PARSE_ERROR).
Public functions: module_action() (sensor_read, http_request_new).
Header guards: PROJECT_MODULE_H or use #pragma once.

decisions.decision: Namespace public headers under include/project_name/; use src/ for implementation; generate compile_commands.json
decisions.rationale: Namespaced headers prevent collisions when installed alongside other libraries. compile_commands.json enables accurate clang-tidy analysis and IDE support.
decisions.details:
Layout:
  src/ — C source files
  include/project_name/ — public headers
  tests/ — test files
  build/ — build output (gitignore)
  .clang-format, .clang-tidy — tool configs
  meson.build (or CMakeLists.txt)

One test file per module. Use compile_commands.json (auto-generated by Meson, -DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON for CMake).

decisions.decision: Use Meson for new projects (clean syntax, integrated dependencies); CMake for ecosystem compatibility
decisions.rationale: Meson provides cleaner syntax, faster builds (Ninja backend), and integrated dependency management via WrapDB. CMake has the largest ecosystem and IDE support. Both are viable — choose based on project needs.
decisions.details:
Meson: Python-like syntax, built-in subproject/wrap system, native cross-compilation. Use meson wrap search/install for dependencies.

CMake: target-based configuration (target_* commands, PRIVATE/PUBLIC/INTERFACE). Use CMakePresets.json. Generate compile_commands.json with -DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON.

Both support sanitizer flags, test integration, and installation targets.
